title: Entanglement distribution: To herald or not to herald
abstract: High-rate, high-fidelity entanglement distribution is essential for the creation of a quantum internet, and spontaneous parametric downconverters (SPDCs) are, at present, the preferred sources of entangled signal-idler photon pairs for transmission to Alice and Bob's quantum nodes. SPDCs using phase-matched spectral islands are especially attractive, in this regard, because they provide wavelength-division multiplexed signal-idler pairs with single-mode temporal behavior. This paper compares the entanglement distribution rates of three islands-based systems. Two use idler detections for heralding: islands-based zero-added-loss multiplexing (ZALM), and an islands-based Sagnac SPDC source with signal-path erasure. The third employs an unheralded Sagnac SPDC source. For 90% or lower heralding efficiencies, ZALM's per-pump-pulse entanglement distribution rate exceeds that of the signal-path erasure source, and both rates are inferior to unheralded operation's when all three systems employ $N_I$ spectral islands and allocate $N_M = N_I$ quantum memories to each pump pulse. These behaviors, however, must be weighed against the three systems' differing equipment requirements, e.g., ZALM requires a pair of perfectly-matched Sagnac sources, which is a significant burden not incurred by the signal-path erasure approach, and both heralded systems will suffer, in comparison with unheralded operation, if they cannot realize high heralding efficiencies.
one_liner: This work benchmarks heralded versus unheralded SPDC entanglement distribution using spectral-island multiplexing architectures for quantum-network links.
why_it_matters: It clarifies when heralding overhead helps or hurts rate, guiding practical source and memory design choices for near-term quantum internet nodes.
detailed_summary: The paper compares three island-based SPDC schemes: zero-added-loss multiplexing (ZALM), a heralded Sagnac source with signal-path erasure, and an unheralded Sagnac source. Using per-pump-pulse entanglement distribution rate as the metric, it analyzes performance when each system uses N_I spectral islands and N_M=N_I memories. For heralding efficiencies up to 90%, ZALM outperforms signal-path erasure, but both heralded approaches are surpassed by unheralded operation under the stated resource allocation. The study also details implementation tradeoffs, including matched-source requirements and sensitivity to achievable heralding efficiency.
platform_or_system: Spectral-island SPDC with Sagnac interferometric sources
main_result: Under equal island/memory allocation, unheralded Sagnac operation yields the highest per-pulse distribution rate, while ZALM beats signal-path erasure for heralding efficiencies at or below 90%.
application_angle: These rate-and-complexity comparisons inform engineering decisions for deployed entanglement links, especially where detector efficiency and hardware matching constraints dominate.

title: Preparing 100-qubit symmetry-protected topological order on a digital quantum computer
abstract: Symmetry-protected topological (SPT) phases extend the Landau paradigm of quantum matter by admitting distinct symmetry-preserving phases that lack any local order parameter. Demonstrating these phases at scale on programmable quantum processors is a key milestone in using quantum hardware to probe emergent many-body phenomena, yet it is impeded by the circuit depth normally required to capture non-trivial entanglement. Here we use a tensor network based approximate quantum compiling (AQC) protocol to construct shallow quantum circuits (18-39 CNOT depth), which prepare 100-site ground states of the spin-1/2 bond-alternating Heisenberg chain in both SPT phases, to 97.9-99.0% fidelity. Upon executing the circuits on IBM quantum hardware, the resulting states exhibit all defining signatures of SPT order including non-local string order for strings of up to length 20, characteristic degeneracies in the entanglement spectrum and clear evidence of symmetry-protected edge modes. The simultaneous observation of these independent diagnostics establishes current quantum computers as versatile platforms for large-scale studies of symmetry-protected quantum matter. More broadly, our results establish a practical foundation for probing non-equilibrium quench dynamics of such systems in regimes that challenge classical computational methods.
one_liner: A tensor-network approximate compiler enables shallow circuits that prepare 100-qubit SPT ground states and reveals multiple SPT diagnostics on IBM hardware.
why_it_matters: It shows current digital quantum processors can realize large-scale topological many-body states with experimentally accessible circuits and robust phase diagnostics.
detailed_summary: The authors use tensor-network-based approximate quantum compiling to generate 18-39 CNOT-depth circuits for 100-site ground states of a bond-alternating spin-1/2 Heisenberg chain in both SPT phases. The compiled states target high fidelity (97.9-99.0%) while keeping depth compatible with noisy hardware. Executions on IBM devices recover hallmark SPT signatures, including non-local string order up to length 20, entanglement-spectrum degeneracies, and symmetry-protected edge-mode evidence. Observing all diagnostics together establishes a scalable hardware route to studying symmetry-protected quantum matter and related dynamics.
main_result: Shallow compiled circuits prepared 100-qubit SPT states with high target fidelity and experimentally exhibited independent SPT diagnostics on real hardware.
application_angle: The method enables practical quantum simulation of correlated/topological phases and quench dynamics in regimes that are difficult for classical computation.

title: Leakage-protected idle operation of a triangular exchange-only spin qubit
abstract: We characterize the coherence of a triangular exchange-only (EO) spin qubit operated at a leakage-protected idle (LPI) point. The triangular geometry enables independent control of all three pairwise exchange interactions, and the LPI condition occurs when these couplings are turned on simultaneously and tuned to equal strength. In this configuration, the exchange interaction induces an energy gap $E_g = 3J/2$ that suppresses leakage from the computational subspace while leaving the qubit state unaffected. We develop procedures to calibrate the LPI point and measure $E_g$, and use these to characterize the qubit dephasing time over a broad range of gap energies. While operating with large always-on exchange couplings exposes the qubit to charge noise, we find that $\tilde{T}_2^*$ still exceeds that of conventional exchange-only spin qubits for $E_g/h < 60$ MHz. The precise control of simultaneous, all-to-all connected exchange demonstrated here presents a natural path towards improving the performance of EO qubits and also enables novel qubit encodings.
one_liner: A triangular exchange-only spin qubit is operated at a leakage-protected idle point where equal always-on exchanges open a leakage-suppressing gap.
why_it_matters: It provides a concrete operating regime that reduces leakage while retaining competitive dephasing, improving viability of semiconductor spin-qubit architectures.
detailed_summary: The work studies a triangular exchange-only qubit with independently tunable pairwise exchanges, focusing on a leakage-protected idle condition where all three couplings are equal and simultaneously on. At this point, exchange creates a gap E_g=3J/2 that suppresses leakage from the computational subspace without rotating the encoded state. The authors calibrate the LPI point, measure E_g, and map dephasing versus gap over a broad range. Despite increased charge-noise exposure from always-on exchange, they report T2* better than conventional exchange-only qubits for E_g/h below 60 MHz.
main_result: Equal all-to-all exchange at the LPI point creates a measurable leakage-protecting gap and maintains improved dephasing performance versus conventional EO operation for moderate gap values.
application_angle: The demonstrated control strategy supports more robust idle intervals and scalable gate scheduling in spin-qubit processors, while enabling alternative encoded-qubit designs.

title: Floquet scars and prethermal fragmentation in a driven spin-one chain
abstract: We study the periodic dynamics of a spin-one chain driven using a square-pulse protocol with amplitude $Q_0$ and frequency $ω_D$. The Hamiltonian of the spin chain hosts a thermodynamically large number of $Z_2$-valued conserved quantities $W_{\ell}$ on the links $\ell$. This allows us to study the Floquet dynamics of this chain within a given sector with fixed values of $W_{\ell}$. For the sector with all $W_{\ell}=1$, we find signatures of quantum many-body scar states for $\hbar ω_D \gg Q_0$; they lead to oscillatory dynamics and fidelity revival for specific initial states. Upon lowering $ω_D$, we find an ergodic regime exhibiting fast thermalization consistent with the prediction of the (Floquet) eigenstate thermalization hypothesis. In addition, we identify special drive frequencies $ω_n^{\ast}= Q_0/(2n \hbar)$ (where $n = 1, 2, 3, \cdots$) at which the Floquet Hamiltonian exhibits prethermal strong Hilbert space fragmentation (HSF) with the largest fragment being ergodic; in contrast, a weak HSF is found at $ω'_n= Q_0/[\hbar(2n+1)]$ (where $n = 0, 1, 2, \cdots$). We also study the sector with $W_{\ell} =\{\cdots 1,1,-1,1,1,-1 \cdots \}$ which shows strong HSF at $ω_n^{\ast}$ but no fragmentation at $ω'_n$. Our analysis indicates that the strong HSF in this sector harbors an integrable largest fragment. We provide numerical support for our analytical and perturbative results using exact-diagonalization (ED) studies on finite chains of length $L\le 24$. Our numerical results for entanglement entropy, fidelity, and correlation functions of the driven chain provide definitive signatures of prethermal strong HSF for both sectors.
one_liner: This work maps driven spin-1 chain dynamics, showing Floquet scars, ergodic windows, and frequency-tuned prethermal Hilbert-space fragmentation.
why_it_matters: It clarifies how drive frequency and symmetry sectors control thermalization versus long-lived constrained dynamics in many-body quantum systems.

title: Variational Quantum Algorithm for Constrained Combinatorial Optimization Problems
abstract: While variational quantum algorithms (VQAs) have demonstrated considerable success in unconstrained optimization, their application to constrained combinatorial problems face a trade-off. Penalty-based methods, despite their circuit simplicity, suffer from a fundamental limitation: inefficient sampling in vast infeasible regions. This often results in suboptimal solutions that violate constraints and impede convergence to high-quality results. In contrast, ansatz-based approaches enforce solution feasibility by design but require complex, problem-specific circuits that are challenging to implement on current noisy intermediate-scale quantum devices. To overcome these limitations, we introduce an alternative VQA whose core innovation lies in a strategically designed loss function. This function offers a dual advantage. First, it is provably guaranteed that its global minimum corresponds uniquely to the optimal feasible solution, as this is achieved by ensuring universally higher loss values for all infeasible solutions. Second, it furnishes distinct computational pathways for feasible versus infeasible regions, thus creating clear and non competing guidance for the optimizer. As a result of these combined features, the algorithm's overall performance is significantly enhanced. Regarding hardware overhead, our design requires adding only an efficient validation oracle module to the penalty-based circuit, resulting in a circuit complexity significantly lower than that of ansatz-based approaches with their custom mixers. To validate the practical efficiency of our method, we empirically demonstrate its effectiveness by solving minimum vertex cover and maximum independent set problems on random graphs of varying small-scale sizes.
one_liner: The paper proposes a VQA loss design for constrained optimization that uniquely favors the optimal feasible solution with low added circuit overhead.
why_it_matters: It offers a practical route to better constrained quantum optimization on NISQ devices without heavy custom ansatz engineering.
